title: Ten years on, can ASEAN finally put the South China Sea ruling to work?
gist: A decade after the 2016 arbitration award invalidated China's nine-dash line, ASEAN keeps its strongest legal instrument off the table — even the 2026 Maritime Cooperation Declaration omits it — leaving the bloc without a common legal baseline just as it builds new maritime institutions under Philippine chairmanship.

insights.claim: A decade after the 2016 South China Sea Arbitration Award invalidated China's nine-dash line and affirmed Philippine EEZ rights, ASEAN has failed to translate the legal victory into operational diplomacy, leaving it symbolic.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: The award remains dormant leverage — its activation would be the signal that ASEAN is hardening its stance.
insights.locator: A decade after the landmark 2016 South China Sea Arbitration Award

insights.claim: The 2016 award's most unrealised value is not compelling Chinese compliance but providing ASEAN a shared legal baseline to coordinate diplomacy and reinforce UNCLOS.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: Frames the award as an internal coordination tool, reframing what 'using' it would mean.
insights.quote: The award's most unrealised value lies not in compelling compliance, but in shaping ASEAN's collective diplomatic strategy.

insights.claim: ASEAN's consensus-based decision-making, combined with members' differing economic priorities and uneven involvement in the disputes, makes strong shared legal language hard, so the award's marginalization was predictable.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: Structural consensus rules cap ASEAN's ability to present a unified legal front against China.

insights.claim: China has demonstrated willingness to impose diplomatic and economic costs on states that challenge its South China Sea position, pushing Southeast Asian governments toward crisis management over legal advocacy.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: Economic coercion risk is why claimants self-censor legally — a recurring constraint on collective action.
insights.locator: China has also demonstrated its willingness

insights.claim: The Philippines under Duterte largely set the award aside in favour of closer ties with Beijing, while Vietnam and Malaysia rarely placed it at the centre of their diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: Claimant behaviour, not just China, hollowed out the ruling — leadership changes can revive or bury it.

insights.claim: Under its 2026 ASEAN chairmanship, Manila is pushing to conclude a legally binding Code of Conduct anchored in UNCLOS by year-end and has established an ASEAN Maritime Centre in the Philippines.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: A binding CoC by end-2026 would be a material regional catalyst — track the negotiation calendar.

insights.claim: The ASEAN Leaders' Declaration on Maritime Cooperation reaffirms UNCLOS but makes no reference to the 2016 arbitration award, leaving ASEAN without a common interpretation of how UNCLOS applies to the South China Sea.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: The pointed omission reveals the limit of ASEAN unity even amid its biggest maritime-institution push.
insights.locator: But the ASEAN Leaders' Declaration on Maritime Cooperation

insights.claim: Embedding the arbitration award into ASEAN Maritime Centre capacity-building and joint statements requires no Chinese acceptance and would strengthen ASEAN's own legal coherence.
insights.speaker: Maxine Nazaree Co
insights.so_what: A low-cost move available to ASEAN unilaterally — its absence signals internal divisions persist.
insights.locator: None of these measures require China's acceptance
